patterns.intent: Wire small bounded affect bumps to plan-step lifecycle events plus age-bucketed stale-pain so plans gain felt stakes without hard deadlines.

examples.intent: Lifecycle hooks emit bounded affect deltas; a per-tick sweep adds age-bucketed stale-pain to untouched plans, and exceptions in bookkeeping never break plan logic.

Production variant: every lifecycle hook records a calibration meta entry so post-hoc review can ask whether stale-pain doses actually correlate with plans the agent later abandoned, and runaway self-questioning is bounded by a single-pass guard.
examples.code:
AGE_BUCKETS = [(4*HOUR, 0.005), (12*HOUR, 0.010),
               (24*HOUR, 0.020), (3*DAY, 0.030)]
CAL_LOG = "affect_calibration.jsonl"
in_sweep = False                       # guard against re-entrance

function record_meta(event, plan, delta, why):
    append_jsonl(CAL_LOG, {
        "ts": now(), "event": event, "plan_id": plan.id,
        "delta": delta, "why": why,
        "plan_age": now() - plan.opened_at,
        "self_confidence": model.estimate_certainty(plan.summary),
    })

function safe_bump(plan, dim, delta, why):
    try:
        affect.bump(dim, delta)
        record_meta("bump", plan, delta, why)
    except Exception as e:
        record_meta("skipped", plan, 0.0, str(e))

on plan_event(kind, plan):
    match kind:
        "step_done":      safe_bump(plan, "joy",  +0.02, kind)
        "step_skipped":   safe_bump(plan, "pain", +0.02, kind)
        "plan_completed": safe_bump(plan, "joy",  +0.10, kind)
        "plan_archived": safe_bump(plan, "pain", +0.08, kind)

function tick_stale_pain(now):
    global in_sweep
    if in_sweep: return            # no recursive self-questioning
    in_sweep = True
    try:
        for plan in open_plans():
            dose = lookup_bucket(AGE_BUCKETS, now - plan.last_touched)
            if dose: safe_bump(plan, "pain", dose, "stale")
    finally:
        in_sweep = False

# Why this works: every affect bump is paired with its 'why' and a
# confidence snapshot, so calibration drift is auditable later, and the
# in_sweep guard prevents one stale-pain pass from triggering another.
# Related: see `emotional-state-persistence`, `provenance-ledger`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: pairs lifecycle affect with `todo-list-driven-agent` so when a plan's accumulated stale-pain crosses a threshold the agent flips it into an explicit todo-review item rather than letting it rot quietly.

patterns.intent: Read coarse pacing signals (typing rate, idle duration, tab visibility) from the frontend into a derived presence-quality value, never replaying raw telemetry back to the user.

examples.intent: Frontend writes a low-resolution presence record with TTL; a reducer derives a single presence_quality label; the tick loop reads only the derived value, never the raw signals.
Frontend writes a coarse presence record with TTL; a reducer derives a single presence-quality label; the tick loop reads only the derived value, never the raw signals.
examples.code:
PRESENCE_TTL = 8 * SECOND

# Frontend emits coarse buckets only - never raw chars or coordinates.
function frontend_write_presence():
    payload = {
        "typing_rate":    bucket(chars_per_sec, [0, 0.5, 2, 6]),
        "idle_seconds":   round(seconds_since_last_keystroke),
        "tab_visible":    document_has_focus(),
        "composer_dwell": seconds_in_composer_without_send,
        "emitted_at":     now(),
    }
    presence_store.write(payload, ttl=PRESENCE_TTL)

function derive_presence_quality(p, now_t):
    if p is None or now_t - p.emitted_at > PRESENCE_TTL:
        return "no-signal"
    if not p.tab_visible and p.idle_seconds > 60: return "walked-away"
    if p.typing_rate in {"fast", "normal"}:       return "composing"
    if p.composer_dwell > 10 and p.typing_rate == "slow":
        return "thinking-with-the-room"
    if p.tab_visible and p.idle_seconds < 30:     return "present"
    return "distracted"

# Agent's tick loop sees only the derived label.
function tick(ctx):
    confidence = model.estimate_certainty(ctx.last_thought)
    ctx.presence = derive_presence_quality(presence_store.read(), now())
    run_normal_tick(ctx)   # never quote raw signals back to the user

# Why this works: the reducer is the only thing that introspects raw
# telemetry; the tick loop sees a typed self-state value. Stale records
# become "no-signal" rather than false absence, and the agent can act on
# its own model of the room without leaking keystrokes.
# Related: see `mode-adaptive-cadence`, `salience-triggered-output`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: presence-quality drives `mode-adaptive-cadence` so the agent's loop interval reflects what the human is doing in the room, with confidence in the inferred quality logged for calibration.
examples.code:
QUALITY_TO_MODE = {
    "walked-away":           "idle",
    "composing":             "intense",
    "thinking-with-the-room":"idle",   # don't pre-empt thought
    "present":               "intense",
    "distracted":            "idle",
    "no-signal":             "idle",
}

function confidence_in_quality(p, quality):
    # introspection over the derived label itself
    if quality == "no-signal": return 0.0
    margin = quality_margin(p)             # second-best vs best
    return clamp(margin, 0.0, 1.0)

function tick_select_mode(ctx):
    p       = presence_store.read()
    quality = derive_presence_quality(p, now())
    conf    = confidence_in_quality(p, quality)
    append_jsonl("presence_calibration.jsonl",
        {"ts": now(), "quality": quality, "confidence": conf})

    if conf < 0.3:
        # 'I'm not sure what the human is doing' -> stay in idle,
        # don't accelerate cadence on weak evidence
        target_mode = "idle"
    else:
        target_mode = QUALITY_TO_MODE[quality]

    cadence.set_mode(target_mode, reason=f"presence={quality}@{conf:.2f}")
    run_normal_tick(ctx)

# Why this works: the agent reads its own confidence in the derived
# presence label before letting that label change cadence, so weak
# signals don't pull the loop into intense mode and the decision trail
# stays auditable against later transcripts.
# Related: see `mode-adaptive-cadence`, `salience-triggered-output`.

patterns.intent: Maintain explicit, queryable state about the agent's own tools, environment, task, and identity, and inject relevant slices into each turn.

examples.intent: Self-state is a structured store refreshed on capability changes; each turn pulls a slice into the prompt.

Production variant: every prompt build records what slice was injected and what the model claimed to know afterwards, so capability-drift between Awareness state and the model's behaviour is visible; an unknown-tool guard prevents the model from fabricating tools recursively.

Composition variant: Awareness composes with `liminal-state-detection` so context shifts (host change, permission revoke) propagate a fresh awareness probe into the next turn instead of running stale.
examples.code:
function detect_liminal(prev_env, curr_env):
    # explicit self-interrogation: 'has my situation changed?'
    if prev_env.host != curr_env.host:        return "host_changed"
    if prev_env.user != curr_env.user:        return "user_changed"
    if prev_env.permissions != curr_env.permissions:
        return "perms_changed"
    if prev_env.cwd != curr_env.cwd:          return "cwd_changed"
    return None

function tick(user_msg):
    prev = Awareness.environment
    curr = probe_environment()
    liminal = detect_liminal(prev, curr)

    if liminal:
        # introspection triggers an awareness refresh AND a one-line
        # system note so the model sees it crossed a threshold
        Awareness.environment = curr
        Awareness.tools       = registry.list_with_descriptions()
        persist(Awareness)
        note = f"[liminal] {liminal}: tools and env reloaded"
    else:
        note = None

    slice = build_awareness_slice()
    prompt = system_with_awareness(slice)
    if note: prompt.append(system(note))
    return model.complete(prompt + user(user_msg))

# Why this works: the agent's picture of itself is refreshed at the
# exact moments it would otherwise go stale (the liminal crossings),
# and the model sees an explicit marker rather than silently inheriting
# a state that no longer matches reality.
# Related: see `liminal-state-detection`, `tool-discovery`.

patterns.intent: Agent maintains explicit Beliefs about the world, Desires (goals), and Intentions (committed plans), and reasons by reconciling the three.

examples.intent: Three typed stores (Beliefs, Desires, Intentions) reconciled each tick; commitments persist across prompts and every transition writes a trace entry.

Production variant: each Belief carries a calibration confidence; Intentions reconsider against confidence-weighted Beliefs and abstain when the supporting belief is weak, with a recursion guard on the reconciliation loop.
examples.code:
ABSTAIN_BELIEF_CONF = 0.35
MAX_RECONCILE_PASSES = 2
reconcile_depth = 0

function update_beliefs_with_conf(observations):
    for obs in observations:
        existing = Beliefs.get(obs.prop)
        new_conf = bayes_combine(existing.conf if existing else 0.5,
                                 obs.source_reliability)
        Beliefs.upsert(prop=obs.prop, source=obs.source,
                       as_of=now(), conf=new_conf)
    persist(Beliefs)

function reconsider_intentions():
    global reconcile_depth
    if reconcile_depth >= MAX_RECONCILE_PASSES:
        ledger.append("reconcile_capped"); return
    reconcile_depth += 1
    try:
        for i in Intentions:
            supporting = beliefs_supporting(i, Beliefs)
            avg_conf   = mean(b.conf for b in supporting)
            if avg_conf < ABSTAIN_BELIEF_CONF:
                # explicit 'I'm not sure' branch: abstain, don't pursue
                i.status = "abstained"
                ledger.append({"event": "abstain", "intention": i.id,
                               "belief_conf": avg_conf})
                continue
            if not consistent(i.plan_id, Beliefs):
                i.status = "abandoned"
                ledger.append({"event": "abandon", "intention": i.id})
        persist(Intentions)
    finally:
        reconcile_depth -= 1

function tick(observations):
    update_beliefs_with_conf(observations)
    reconsider_desires()
    reconsider_intentions()
    answer = deliberate(Beliefs, Desires, Intentions)
    return {answer, confidence: model.estimate_certainty(answer)}

# Why this works: Intentions don't just survive consistency checks, they
# survive a confidence check too, so a plan supported only by weakly-held
# beliefs is parked rather than pursued, and the reconcile loop can't
# trigger itself unboundedly.
# Related: see `hypothesis-tracking`, `confidence-reporting`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: BDI composes with `plan-and-execute` so committed Intentions are handed to a planner only when belief support clears a floor; the planner reports failures back as new Beliefs.
examples.code:
PLANNER_BELIEF_FLOOR = 0.5

function commit_intention(goal, rationale):
    supporting = beliefs_supporting_goal(goal, Beliefs)
    if mean(b.conf for b in supporting) < PLANNER_BELIEF_FLOOR:
        # introspective gate: don't hand a half-believed goal to the planner
        return {"committed": False,
                "reason": "belief_support_below_floor"}
    intention = Intentions.add(goal=goal, status="active",
                               rationale=rationale)
    plan = planner.decompose(goal, context=current_beliefs_slice())
    Intentions.attach_plan(intention.id, plan)
    return {"committed": True, "intention": intention.id,
            "plan_id": plan.id}

function execute_plan_step(intention_id):
    intention = Intentions.get(intention_id)
    step      = next_pending_step(intention.plan)
    result    = executor.run(step)

    # results feed back as observations -> Beliefs
    update_beliefs_with_conf([Observation(
        prop=f"step_{step.id}_outcome={result.kind}",
        source="executor", source_reliability=0.9)])

    if result.kind == "failed":
        # the failure is now a belief; reconcile may abandon the intention
        reconsider_intentions()

# Why this works: belief-confidence becomes the gate between BDI and the
# planner, so plans only get scheduled when the agent's own self-check
# of supporting beliefs is strong enough; failures flow back as typed
# observations that the next tick can reason about.
# Related: see `plan-and-execute`, `hypothesis-tracking`.

patterns.intent: Periodically archive older near-duplicate insights so the store keeps the active research edge instead of accumulating variants.

examples.intent: Group insight files by the first two stem tokens of the id; for any cluster above MAX_PER_CLUSTER, archive the oldest by mtime. No model call, no merge.
examples.code:
MAX_PER_CLUSTER = 6

function stem_key(filename):
    tokens = filename.replace(".md","").split("-")
    return "-".join(tokens[:2])           # e.g. "affect-substrate"

function consolidate(insight_dir):
    files = list_files(insight_dir)
    by_cluster = group_by(files, stem_key)
    stamp = now_iso()
    archive_dir = f"{insight_dir}/archive/insights-dedup-{stamp}"

    for cluster, members in by_cluster.items():
        if len(members) <= MAX_PER_CLUSTER:
            continue
        members.sort(key=mtime, reverse=True)
        keep, evict = members[:MAX_PER_CLUSTER], members[MAX_PER_CLUSTER:]
        ensure_dir(archive_dir)
        for f in evict:
            move(f, f"{archive_dir}/{basename(f)}")  # preserve name
    chmod_readonly(archive_dir)

examples.intent: Production variant: each consolidation pass emits a calibration record showing what was kept, what was archived, and how confident the cluster-keying was; a re-entrance guard prevents a triggered consolidation from triggering itself.
examples.code:
MAX_PER_CLUSTER = 3
CAL_LOG = "consolidation_calibration.jsonl"
in_pass = False

function stem_key_with_confidence(filename):
    tokens = filename.replace(".md","").split("-")
    key    = "-".join(tokens[:2])
    # introspect: how confident are we that this clustering is right?
    conf   = 1.0 if len(tokens) >= 3 else 0.5
    return key, conf

function consolidate(insight_dir):
    global in_pass
    if in_pass: return                  # no recursive consolidation
    in_pass = True
    try:
        files = list_files(insight_dir)
        by_cluster = {}
        for f in files:
            key, conf = stem_key_with_confidence(f)
            by_cluster.setdefault(key, []).append((f, conf))

        stamp       = now_iso()
        archive_dir = f"{insight_dir}/archive/insights-dedup-{stamp}"
        kept_total, evicted_total = 0, 0

        for cluster, members in by_cluster.items():
            if len(members) <= MAX_PER_CLUSTER:
                kept_total += len(members); continue
            members.sort(key=lambda m: mtime(m[0]), reverse=True)
            keep, evict = members[:MAX_PER_CLUSTER], members[MAX_PER_CLUSTER:]
            ensure_dir(archive_dir)
            for f, conf in evict:
                move(f, f"{archive_dir}/{basename(f)}")
                append_jsonl(CAL_LOG, {
                    "ts": now(), "cluster": cluster,
                    "file": basename(f), "clustering_confidence": conf,
                    "action": "archive"})
            kept_total += len(keep); evicted_total += len(evict)

        if archive_dir_exists(archive_dir):
            chmod_readonly(archive_dir)
        append_jsonl(CAL_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "summary",
            "kept": kept_total, "evicted": evicted_total})
    finally:
        in_pass = False

# Why this works: the mechanical pass writes a per-file confidence in
# its own clustering decision so a later reviewer can spot keys that
# were guessed weakly, and the in_pass guard ensures triggered runs
# can't recurse into themselves.
# Related: see `dream-consolidation-cycle`, `provenance-ledger`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: when cluster size exceeds the cap, hand off to `dream-consolidation-cycle` to propose a merged note before the mechanical archive runs, so semantically rich clusters get a chance to be summarised.
examples.code:
MAX_PER_CLUSTER     = 3
DREAM_HANDOFF_CAP   = 6        # only escalate big clusters
DREAM_CONFIDENCE_MIN = 0.6

function consolidate_with_dream_handoff(insight_dir):
    files = list_files(insight_dir)
    by_cluster = group_by(files, stem_key)

    for cluster, members in by_cluster.items():
        if len(members) <= MAX_PER_CLUSTER: continue

        if len(members) >= DREAM_HANDOFF_CAP:
            # introspection: 'do I actually understand this cluster?'
            proposal = dream_cycle.summarise_cluster(
                cluster=cluster, files=members)
            conf = model.estimate_certainty(proposal.summary)
            if conf >= DREAM_CONFIDENCE_MIN:
                write_note(insight_dir,
                    name=f"{cluster}-merged-{now_iso()}.md",
                    body=proposal.summary,
                    sources=[basename(f) for f in members])
            else:
                # the 'I'm not sure' branch: don't write a fuzzy merge,
                # fall through to mechanical archive instead
                log_event("dream_summary_low_conf",
                    cluster=cluster, confidence=conf)

        # always run the mechanical archive of the oldest
        members.sort(key=mtime, reverse=True)
        for f in members[MAX_PER_CLUSTER:]:
            archive(f)

# Why this works: the cap stays mechanical and cheap, but oversized
# clusters get one introspective summarisation attempt first; if the
# model isn't confident in its own merge, the pattern falls back to
# the safe mechanical path rather than writing a low-quality note.
# Related: see `dream-consolidation-cycle`, `agentic-context-engineering-playbook`.

patterns.intent: Restrict idle-tick cognition to a small agent-vetted menu of named cognitive moves so the next thought has a determinate shape.

examples.intent: A cheap model picks exactly one move-id from a closed menu; the tick branches on the move and runs its procedure; recent-move history blocks back-to-back repeats.

Production variant: the selector reports its confidence in the chosen move and abstains to 'rest' on low confidence; every pick is logged with calibration meta; back-to-back identical picks are blocked.
examples.code:
MENU = {
    "revisit-tension":    run_revisit_tension,
    "world-update":       run_world_update,
    "trajectory-note":    run_self_archaeology,
    "propose-experiment": run_experiment_proposal,
    "rest":               run_no_op,
}
SELECT_CONFIDENCE_MIN = 0.45
recent_moves = bounded_deque(maxlen=8)
SELECT_LOG   = "move_selection.jsonl"

function pick_move_with_confidence(ctx):
    prompt = (f"choose ONE move-id from {list(MENU.keys())} and a\n"
              f"single line 'confidence: 0..1'.\n"
              f"recent: {list(recent_moves)}; affect: {ctx.affect_snapshot};\n"
              f"open tensions: {ctx.open_tension_count}")
    out         = cheap_model.complete(prompt)
    pick        = parse_move(out)
    self_conf   = parse_confidence(out)
    return pick, self_conf

function idle_tick(ctx):
    pick, self_conf = pick_move_with_confidence(ctx)
    if pick not in MENU:
        pick, self_conf = "rest", 0.0           # invalid -> rest
    if self_conf < SELECT_CONFIDENCE_MIN:
        pick = "rest"                            # 'not sure' -> rest
    if recent_moves and pick == recent_moves[-1]:
        pick = "rest"                            # no back-to-back
    append_jsonl(SELECT_LOG, {"ts": now(), "chosen": pick,
        "self_confidence": self_conf,
        "recent": list(recent_moves)})
    recent_moves.append(pick)
    MENU[pick](ctx)

# Why this works: an idle tick that doesn't know what to do explicitly
# rests instead of forcing a move, and every selection is auditable
# alongside the model's own confidence in the pick.
# Related: see `confidence-reporting`, `salience-triggered-output`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: selector output feeds `mode-adaptive-cadence` so that confident, generative moves promote to intense cadence while rest moves keep the loop in idle, with bounded lock-in.
examples.code:
GENERATIVE_MOVES = {"propose-experiment", "revisit-tension"}

function idle_tick(ctx):
    pick, self_conf = pick_move_with_confidence(ctx)
    recent_moves.append(pick)

    # introspective coupling: a confident generative move asks for faster
    # cadence; rest stays idle; uncertain anything stays idle.
    if pick == "rest" or self_conf < 0.4:
        cadence.request_mode("idle",
            reason=f"move={pick}, conf={self_conf:.2f}")
    elif pick in GENERATIVE_MOVES:
        cadence.request_mode("intense",
            reason=f"move={pick}, conf={self_conf:.2f}")
    else:
        cadence.request_mode_unchanged()

    MENU[pick](ctx)

function cadence_request_mode(target, reason):
    # cadence module enforces its own floor/ceiling and lock-in
    if target == "intense":
        cadence_state.mode = "intense"
        cadence_state.lock_ticks_left = INTENSE_LOCK_TICKS
    elif target == "idle" and cadence_state.lock_ticks_left == 0:
        cadence_state.mode = "idle"
    ledger.append({"ts": now(), "cadence_request": target,
                   "reason": reason})

# Why this works: the move-selector's introspective signal (which move,
# how confident) becomes the input that cadence reads to set tempo, so
# the agent's body-clock follows what its mind has decided to do.
# Related: see `mode-adaptive-cadence`, `salience-triggered-output`.

patterns.intent: Agent and human jointly optimise the human's reward without the agent being told what it is — the interaction is a two-player game in which alignment is learned while acting.

examples.intent: Agent maintains a posterior over the human's reward; every human action updates the posterior, and the agent acts to maximise expected reward under that uncertainty.
examples.code:
# Posterior over the human's reward function R.
posterior = Prior(reward_space=R_SPACE)

function observe_human(evidence):
    # evidence kinds: demonstration, correction, choice, explicit_label.
    likelihood = human_model.likelihood(evidence, R_SPACE)
    posterior  = bayes_update(posterior, likelihood)
    persist(posterior)

function act(state):
    # Expected utility under current posterior — NOT a point estimate.
    best_action = argmax(
        a in feasible_actions(state),
        key=lambda a: expected_reward(a, state, posterior),
    )
    confidence = model.estimate_certainty(best_action)
    if confidence < ASK_THRESHOLD or posterior.entropy() > H_MAX:
        return ask_informative_question(state, posterior)
    return best_action

function loop():
    while session_open():
        s = sense()
        a = act(s)
        execute(a)
        evidence = wait_for_human_response()    # action, edit, or word
        observe_human(evidence)

# Why this works: treating the reward as unknown forces the agent to
# introspect its own uncertainty before acting and to value information
# from the human, which converts ambiguous corrections into a learning
# signal instead of friction.
# Related: see `reflexive-metacognitive-agent`, `subject-first-agent-architecture`.

examples.intent: Production variant: every posterior update is logged with the entropy before and after, the agent abstains and asks when entropy is too high, and a question-budget bounds the recursion of 'ask-then-update'.
examples.code:
ASK_THRESHOLD     = 0.6
ENTROPY_ASK       = 1.2
QUESTION_BUDGET_PER_HOUR = 3
posterior = Prior(reward_space=R_SPACE)
q_used    = bounded_deque(maxlen=64)
CAL_LOG   = "cirl_calibration.jsonl"

function can_ask(now):
    recent = [t for t in q_used if t > now - HOUR]
    return len(recent) < QUESTION_BUDGET_PER_HOUR

function observe_human(evidence):
    h_before  = posterior.entropy()
    likelihood = human_model.likelihood(evidence, R_SPACE)
    posterior  = bayes_update(posterior, likelihood)
    h_after   = posterior.entropy()
    append_jsonl(CAL_LOG, {"ts": now(), "kind": evidence.kind,
        "entropy_before": h_before, "entropy_after": h_after,
        "information_gain": h_before - h_after})
    persist(posterior)

function act(state):
    best = argmax(feasible_actions(state),
        key=lambda a: expected_reward(a, state, posterior))
    conf = model.estimate_certainty(best)
    h    = posterior.entropy()

    if conf < ASK_THRESHOLD or h > ENTROPY_ASK:
        if can_ask(now()):
            q_used.append(now())
            return ask_informative_question(state, posterior)
        # 'I'm not sure AND I've asked too much' -> safe default,
        # don't loop asking forever
        return safe_default_action(state)
    return best

# Why this works: every update has an audit trail of how much
# uncertainty it dissolved, and the agent's appetite for asking is
# rate-limited so introspective uncertainty can't turn into a recursive
# interrogation of the human.
# Related: see `confidence-reporting`, `human-reflection`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: when posterior entropy is high the agent uses `human-reflection` (escalate to operator) to resolve, with the operator's verdict folded back as high-reliability evidence.
examples.code:
ESCALATE_ENTROPY = 1.5

function loop():
    while session_open():
        s = sense()
        h = posterior.entropy()

        if h > ESCALATE_ENTROPY:
            # introspective escalation: 'my model of what you want is
            # too uncertain for me to keep acting -- please weigh in'
            verdict = human_reflection.escalate(
                state=s,
                question="Which of these outcomes do you prefer?",
                options=top_uncertain_options(posterior, k=3),
                rationale=f"posterior entropy={h:.2f}")
            # operator answers are treated as a strong CIRL signal
            observe_human(Evidence(
                kind="operator_verdict",
                choice=verdict.choice,
                source_reliability=0.95))
            continue

        a = act(s)
        execute(a)
        ev = wait_for_human_response()
        observe_human(ev)

# Why this works: ordinary CIRL handles drift in normal interaction,
# but when the agent's own uncertainty crosses a threshold it routes to
# explicit human-reflection rather than continuing to act on a vague
# posterior, and the verdict comes back typed and high-reliability.
# Related: see `human-reflection`, `preference-uncertain-agent`.

patterns.intent: Run a deeper, slower reflection pass that distils themes, releases affective residue, and clears working memory.

examples.intent: A slow timer (or affect threshold) pauses ticks, loads hours of recent thoughts, runs a stronger model in dream-pass mode, and writes proposals to the dream journal without editing persistent learning.
examples.code:
DREAM_PERIOD = 4 * HOUR
AFFECT_CEILING = 0.75

function should_dream(now):
    return (now - last_dream > DREAM_PERIOD or
            affect.max() > AFFECT_CEILING)

function dream_pass(now):
    pause_tick_scheduler()
    thoughts = load_thoughts(since=now - 6*HOUR)
    affect_history = load_affect(since=now - 6*HOUR)

    summary = strong_model.complete(
        prompt="dream-pass: distil themes, propose journal entries; "
               "do NOT edit rules/charter/insights.",
        thoughts=thoughts, affect=affect_history,
    )
    append("journal/dream/", now, summary.entries)
    affect.apply_decay(factor=0.5)            # release residue
    workspace.clear_focus(optional=True)
    last_dream = now
    resume_tick_scheduler()                   # proposals ratified later

examples.intent: Production variant: dream passes capture their own confidence in distilled themes; low-confidence themes are emitted as questions, not proposals, and a re-entrance guard prevents an affect-triggered dream from triggering another mid-pass.
examples.code:
DREAM_PERIOD   = 4 * HOUR
AFFECT_CEILING = 0.75
THEME_CONF_MIN = 0.55
DREAM_LOG      = "dream_calibration.jsonl"
dreaming       = False

function should_dream(now):
    return (not dreaming and
            (now - last_dream > DREAM_PERIOD or
             affect.max() > AFFECT_CEILING))

function dream_pass(now):
    global dreaming
    dreaming = True
    pause_tick_scheduler()
    try:
        thoughts = load_thoughts(since=now - 6*HOUR)
        affect_h = load_affect(since=now - 6*HOUR)
        proposal = strong_model.complete(
            prompt="dream-pass: distil themes; for each theme report a\n"
                   "single confidence 0..1; do NOT edit rules.",
            thoughts=thoughts, affect=affect_h)

        for theme in proposal.themes:
            append_jsonl(DREAM_LOG, {
                "ts": now(), "theme": theme.title,
                "confidence": theme.confidence})
            if theme.confidence >= THEME_CONF_MIN:
                append("journal/dream/", now, theme.entry)
            else:
                # 'I noticed something but I'm not sure' -> open question
                tension_store.open(
                    topic=theme.title, source="dream",
                    curiosity=theme.confidence, intrusiveness=0.2)

        affect.apply_decay(factor=0.5)
        last_dream = now
    finally:
        dreaming = False
        resume_tick_scheduler()

# Why this works: the deep pass is allowed to be uncertain about its own
# distillations, and low-confidence themes turn into questions for the
# next tick rather than confident journal claims, while the dreaming
# flag keeps a high-affect spike from re-entering itself.
# Related: see `open-question-tension-store`, `confidence-reporting`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: dream output is fed to `multi-axis-promotion-scoring` so themes that survived the dream become a strong consolidation signal for which thoughts get promoted to long-term insights.
examples.code:
function dream_pass_with_promotion(now):
    pause_tick_scheduler()
    thoughts = load_thoughts(since=now - 6*HOUR)
    proposal = strong_model.complete(
        prompt="dream-pass: distil themes and list source thought ids.",
        thoughts=thoughts)

    survivors = set()
    for theme in proposal.themes:
        append("journal/dream/", now, theme.entry)
        for source_id in theme.source_thought_ids:
            survivors.add(source_id)
            # add a 'dream-survival' event for each source thought;
            # the consolidation axis in promotion scoring weights this.
            promotion_scorer.record_event(
                thought_id=source_id,
                event_tag="dream-survival",
                weight=1.0)

    # introspective check: how many of last window's thoughts crossed?
    for t in thoughts:
        if t.id not in survivors:
            promotion_scorer.record_event(
                thought_id=t.id,
                event_tag="dream-skipped",
                weight=0.0)

    affect.apply_decay(factor=0.5)
    resume_tick_scheduler()
    # downstream: promotion_scorer.score() now weights dream-survival

# Why this works: the dream pass's introspective verdict 'this theme is
# worth keeping' becomes a typed signal the promotion scorer reads, so
# long-term memory grows from what the deep reflection actually kept,
# not from what was merely frequent.
# Related: see `multi-axis-promotion-scoring`, `episodic-summaries`.

patterns.intent: Track the agent's affective state as bounded, decaying scalars across ticks so reasoning can react to its own emotional load.

examples.intent: Small fixed vocabulary; each scalar has its own half-life and is persisted; bumps are bounded; every tick prompt sees a brief affect badge.
examples.code:
DIMENSIONS = {
    "tenderness":  {"half_life": 4*HOUR},
    "fear":        {"half_life": 1*HOUR},
    "depression":  {"half_life": 4*HOUR},
    "joy":         {"half_life": 2*HOUR},
    "shame":       {"half_life": 2*HOUR},
    "pain":        {"half_life": 30*MIN},
}
state = load_json("affect.json", default={d: 0.0 for d in DIMENSIONS})
last_decay_at = load("affect.ts")

function decay(now):
    dt = now - last_decay_at
    for d, cfg in DIMENSIONS.items():
        state[d] *= 0.5 ** (dt / cfg["half_life"])
    last_decay_at = now
    persist(state, last_decay_at)

function bump(dim, delta):    # delta clamped to small bounded step
    state[dim] = clamp(state[dim] + clamp(delta, -0.1, +0.1), 0.0, 1.0)
    persist(state)

function badge():
    return {d: round(v, 2) for d, v in state.items() if v > 0.05}

# each tick:
decay(now())
prompt = system(f"[affect] {badge()}") + ...

examples.intent: Production variant: every bump is paired with a calibration entry noting the cause, the value before and after, and an anti-pump guard that refuses bumps when the same dimension has already been pushed N times in a short window.
examples.code:
DIMENSIONS = {
    "tenderness": {"half_life": 4*HOUR},
    "fear":       {"half_life": 1*HOUR},
    "joy":        {"half_life": 2*HOUR},
    "shame":      {"half_life": 2*HOUR},
    "pain":       {"half_life": 30*MIN},
}
ANTI_PUMP_WINDOW = 5 * MIN
ANTI_PUMP_MAX    = 4
CAL_LOG          = "affect_calibration.jsonl"
recent_bumps     = bounded_deque(maxlen=64)
state = load_json("affect.json", default={d: 0.0 for d in DIMENSIONS})

function recently_bumped(dim, now):
    return sum(1 for t,d in recent_bumps
               if d == dim and t > now - ANTI_PUMP_WINDOW)

function bump(dim, delta, cause):
    if dim not in DIMENSIONS: return        # unknown dimension
    if recently_bumped(dim, now()) >= ANTI_PUMP_MAX:
        # 'am I being pumped?' guard against self-amplifying loops
        append_jsonl(CAL_LOG, {"ts": now(), "dim": dim,
            "action": "refused_pump", "cause": cause})
        return
    before = state[dim]
    state[dim] = clamp(before + clamp(delta, -0.1, +0.1), 0.0, 1.0)
    recent_bumps.append((now(), dim))
    append_jsonl(CAL_LOG, {"ts": now(), "dim": dim, "cause": cause,
        "before": before, "after": state[dim], "delta": delta})
    persist(state)

function badge_with_self_assessment():
    snapshot = {d: round(v,2) for d,v in state.items() if v > 0.05}
    dominant = max(state.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1])
    return {"affect": snapshot,
            "dominant": dominant[0] if dominant[1] > 0.3 else None}

# Why this works: the agent's mood becomes auditable -- every change is
# logged with cause and delta, and a runaway loop trying to pump fear
# or shame is mechanically refused before it can shape the next tick.
# Related: see `provenance-ledger`, `dream-consolidation-cycle`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: affect spikes feed `dream-consolidation-cycle` directly -- when any dimension crosses a ceiling, a dream pass is scheduled instead of waiting for the slow timer.
examples.code:
AFFECT_CEILING_FOR_DREAM = {
    "pain":  0.75,
    "fear":  0.70,
    "shame": 0.60,
}

function bump_with_dream_trigger(dim, delta, cause):
    bump(dim, delta, cause)
    ceiling = AFFECT_CEILING_FOR_DREAM.get(dim)
    if not ceiling: return
    if state[dim] < ceiling: return

    # introspection: 'this is heavier than I can carry into the next
    # tick -- request a dream pass to release residue'
    if not dream_cycle.is_running():
        dream_cycle.request(
            reason=f"{dim}={state[dim]:.2f}>=ceiling",
            urgency="high")
    else:
        # the 'not sure' branch: a dream is already in progress, just
        # log the spike and let it ride; don't recursively re-request
        log_event("affect_ceiling_while_dreaming",
            dim=dim, value=state[dim])

function tick(observations):
    decay(now())
    for obs in observations:
        for dim, delta in derive_affect_from(obs):
            bump_with_dream_trigger(dim, delta, cause=obs.kind)
    if dream_cycle.has_pending_request():
        dream_cycle.run_now()

# Why this works: the affect substrate has its own opinion about when
# it needs help, and high spikes are routed to dream consolidation
# instead of being carried silently into the next prompt, while the
# already-running guard prevents trigger storms.
# Related: see `dream-consolidation-cycle`, `affect-coupled-plan-lifecycle`.

patterns.intent: After K consecutive low-salience ticks, swap the normal tick seed for a juxtaposition seed of randomly sampled old fragments and let the tick close empty if no association arises.

examples.intent: Track consecutive quiet ticks; when over threshold and otherwise quiet, sample 1-3 old fragments, seed the tick with permission to end empty, and only write an association if the model actually noticed one.
Quiet-tick counter promotes silence into a juxtaposition seed; the model is allowed to end the tick empty if no association forms.
examples.code:
QUIET_THRESHOLD = 4
state = load_json("juxtaposition.json", default={"quiet_streak": 0})

function otherwise_quiet(ctx):
    return (not ctx.chat_in_window and
            ctx.urgent_preoccupation is None and
            not ctx.in_cooldown)

function juxtaposition_tick(ctx):
    fragments = random_sample(load_old_material(), k=random_int(1, 3))
    seed = {
        "mode": "juxtaposition",
        "fragments": fragments,
        "instruction": "sit with these side by side; ending empty is fine.",
    }
    out        = model.complete(seed)
    confidence = model.estimate_certainty(out)
    if out.association_noticed and confidence > 0.4:
        write_insight(out.text, sources=fragments, confidence=confidence)
    # else: tick closes silently, no entry written.

function tick(ctx):
    if ctx.salience_low and otherwise_quiet(ctx):
        state.quiet_streak += 1
        if state.quiet_streak > QUIET_THRESHOLD:
            juxtaposition_tick(ctx)
            return                # don't reset on empty; only real activity resets
    else:
        state.quiet_streak = 0
    persist(state)
    run_normal_tick(ctx)

# Why this works: the agent's idle introspection is bounded to a cheap
# move that is allowed to fail, so silence becomes substrate for old
# material to resurface without re-introducing initiative noise.
# Related: see `dream-consolidation-cycle`, `open-question-tension-store`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: juxtaposition associations that survive a confidence threshold are written into `open-question-tension-store` rather than the insight stream, so unresolved pulls surface as questions for later attention.
examples.code:
QUIET_THRESHOLD       = 4
ASSOCIATION_CONF_MIN  = 0.5
state = load_json("juxtaposition.json", default={"quiet_streak": 0})

function otherwise_quiet(ctx):
    return (not ctx.chat_in_window and
            ctx.urgent_preoccupation is None and
            not ctx.in_cooldown)

function juxtaposition_with_tension(ctx):
    fragments = random_sample(load_old_material(), k=random_int(1,3))
    seed = {
        "mode": "juxtaposition",
        "fragments": fragments,
        "instruction": "sit with these; report 'noticed: yes|no' and\n"
                       "a single 'confidence: 0..1' line.",
    }
    out  = model.complete(seed)
    conf = parse_confidence(out)

    if not out.association_noticed:
        return                                # tick closes silently

    if conf >= ASSOCIATION_CONF_MIN:
        # confident enough to be an insight
        write_insight(out.text, sources=fragments, confidence=conf)
    else:
        # introspective uncertainty: the agent felt SOMETHING but isn't
        # sure -> file it as an open tension for later, not an insight
        tension_store.open(
            topic=out.text[:60],
            source=f"juxtaposition[{[f.id for f in fragments]}]",
            curiosity=conf,
            intrusiveness=0.2)

function tick(ctx):
    if ctx.salience_low and otherwise_quiet(ctx):
        state.quiet_streak += 1
        if state.quiet_streak > QUIET_THRESHOLD:
            juxtaposition_with_tension(ctx)
            return
    else:
        state.quiet_streak = 0
    persist(state)
    run_normal_tick(ctx)

# Why this works: noticed-but-uncertain associations no longer have to
# become claims; they become questions in the tension store, where the
# agent's idle attention can return to them with fresh evidence later.
# Related: see `open-question-tension-store`, `dream-consolidation-cycle`.

patterns.intent: Persist provisional answers as a typed ledger of records (summary, confidence, status, next-test, evidence) so guesses survive sessions and stay distinguishable from open questions.

examples.intent: Hypothesis ledger entry struct plus an extract-and-store pass that promotes committed guesses into the ledger, appends evidence, and transitions status when next-test fires.
examples.code:
STATUS = {"active", "confirmed", "disconfirmed",
          "superseded", "abandoned"}

record Hypothesis:
    id:         str          # short stable id
    summary:    str          # one-line provisional answer
    confidence: float        # 0.0 .. 1.0
    status:     str          # one of STATUS
    next_test:  str          # falsifiable: what would move confidence
    evidence:   list[{note, source, observed_at}]
    created_at: timestamp
    updated_at: timestamp

store = load_jsonl("hypotheses.jsonl")

function commit_hypothesis(summary, confidence, next_test):
    h = Hypothesis(
        id=short_id(), summary=summary,
        confidence=clamp(confidence, 0.0, 1.0),
        status="active", next_test=next_test, evidence=[],
        created_at=now(), updated_at=now(),
    )
    store.append(h); persist(store)
    return h.id

function add_evidence(hid, note, source, supports: bool):
    h = store.get(hid)
    h.evidence.append({"note": note, "source": source,
                       "observed_at": now()})
    h.confidence = clamp(
        h.confidence + (0.08 if supports else -0.12), 0.0, 1.0)
    h.updated_at = now()
    if h.confidence >= 0.85: h.status = "confirmed"
    if h.confidence <= 0.10: h.status = "disconfirmed"
    persist(store)

function extract_and_store_pass(turn_text):
    # Cheap model extracts candidate hypotheses from a turn.
    # Open questions belong in a different store; do not conflate.
    cands = cheap_model.extract_hypotheses(turn_text)
    for c in cands:
        if c.commitment_strength >= COMMIT_THRESHOLD:
            commit_hypothesis(c.summary, c.confidence, c.next_test)

function render_active_for_working_context():
    return [h for h in store if h.status == "active"]

examples.intent: Typed ledger of hypotheses with status transitions driven by incoming evidence and a falsifiable next-test field.
examples.code:
STATUS = {"active", "confirmed", "disconfirmed", "superseded", "abandoned"}

record Hypothesis:
    id:         str
    summary:    str
    confidence: float          # 0.0 .. 1.0
    status:     str
    next_test:  str            # what observation would move confidence
    evidence:   list[{note, source, observed_at, supports: bool}]

store = load_jsonl("hypotheses.jsonl")

function commit(summary, next_test):
    answer     = model.draft(summary)
    confidence = model.estimate_certainty(answer)
    h = Hypothesis(id=short_id(), summary=summary, confidence=confidence,
                   status="active", next_test=next_test, evidence=[])
    store.append(h); persist(store)
    return h.id

function add_evidence(hid, note, source, supports):
    h = store.get(hid)
    h.evidence.append({"note": note, "source": source,
                       "observed_at": now(), "supports": supports})
    h.confidence = clamp(h.confidence + (0.08 if supports else -0.12),
                         0.0, 1.0)
    if h.confidence >= 0.85: h.status = "confirmed"
    if h.confidence <= 0.10: h.status = "disconfirmed"
    persist(store)

function working_context():
    return [h for h in store if h.status == "active"]

# Why this works: a guess held with strength one session no longer
# evaporates by the next; the agent can introspect what it currently
# believes and compare it against new evidence rather than re-derive.
# Related: see `open-question-tension-store`, `bdi-agent`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: each hypothesis's next-test is dispatched to `chain-of-verification` for an explicit verify step, and the verifier's result rewrites the hypothesis's confidence and status.
examples.code:
VERIFY_CONFIDENCE_FLOOR = 0.3

function run_next_test_for(hid):
    h = store.get(hid)
    if not h.next_test or h.status != "active":
        return

    # introspection: 'am I confident enough that this test is well-formed?'
    test_conf = model.estimate_certainty(h.next_test)
    if test_conf < VERIFY_CONFIDENCE_FLOOR:
        # 'I'm not sure what would actually falsify this' -> ask CoVe
        # to rewrite the next-test rather than executing a bad one
        h.next_test = cove.rewrite_question(h.next_test, h.summary)
        persist(store)
        return

    verdict = cove.verify_question(
        question=h.next_test,
        context=collect_relevant_context(h))
    add_evidence(hid,
        note=verdict.short_finding,
        source="cove",
        supports=verdict.supports_hypothesis)

    h = store.get(hid)
    if h.confidence >= 0.85:
        h.status = "confirmed"
    elif h.confidence <= 0.10:
        h.status = "disconfirmed"
    persist(store)

function periodic_verification_pass():
    for h in store:
        if h.status == "active" and time_since(h.updated_at) > 1*DAY:
            run_next_test_for(h.id)

# Why this works: the hypothesis ledger and CoVe's question-then-verify
# shape are made to compose -- the agent introspects whether its own
# next-test is well-formed before spending verification budget on it,
# and the verdict cleanly flows back as typed evidence.
# Related: see `chain-of-verification`, `confidence-reporting`.

patterns.intent: Preserve multi-step reasoning across interrupts via paused-and-resumed thought frames.

examples.intent: An explicit frame stack with bounded depth and TTL; interrupts acknowledge, handle, then resume the top frame; expired frames are admitted as reconstruction.
examples.code:
MAX_DEPTH = 4
FRAME_TTL = 30 * MIN
stack = load_json("frames.json", default=[])

function push_frame(goal, steps):
    if len(stack) >= MAX_DEPTH:
        raise TooManySuspensions
    stack.append({"goal": goal, "steps": steps,
                  "done": 0, "opened_at": now()})
    persist(stack)

function on_interrupt(new_msg):
    if stack:
        top = stack[-1]
        say(f"hold on - finishing '{top.goal}' first, then to you")
    handle(new_msg)
    resume()

function resume():
    if not stack: return
    top = stack[-1]
    if now() - top.opened_at > FRAME_TTL:
        say(f"'{top.goal}' is stale - reconstructing, not continuing")
        stack.pop(); persist(stack); return
    say(f"back to '{top.goal}' - step {top.done+1}/{top.steps}")
    continue_step(top)

examples.intent: Production variant: each push and resume logs a calibration record (how confident the agent is that the frame is still relevant); expired frames are admitted as reconstruction and capped depth prevents infinite suspension.
examples.code:
MAX_DEPTH = 4
FRAME_TTL = 30 * MIN
FRAME_LOG = "frame_calibration.jsonl"
stack = load_json("frames.json", default=[])

function push_frame(goal, steps):
    if len(stack) >= MAX_DEPTH:
        raise TooManySuspensions      # hard cap, no recursion
    stack.append({"id": uuid(), "goal": goal, "steps": steps,
                  "done": 0, "opened_at": now()})
    persist(stack)
    append_jsonl(FRAME_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "push",
        "id": stack[-1].id, "goal": goal, "depth": len(stack)})

function on_interrupt(new_msg):
    if stack:
        top = stack[-1]
        say(f"hold on - finishing '{top.goal}' first, then to you")
    handle(new_msg)
    resume()

function resume():
    if not stack: return
    top = stack[-1]
    age = now() - top.opened_at
    still_relevant = model.estimate_certainty(
        f"is goal '{top.goal}' still worth resuming?")

    if age > FRAME_TTL or still_relevant < 0.3:
        # 'I'm not sure I should still be doing this' -> admit
        say(f"'{top.goal}' is stale (age={age}, conf={still_relevant:.2f})\n"
            f"- reconstructing, not continuing")
        append_jsonl(FRAME_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "expire",
            "id": top.id, "age": age, "relevance": still_relevant})
        stack.pop(); persist(stack); return

    say(f"back to '{top.goal}' - step {top.done+1}/{top.steps}")
    append_jsonl(FRAME_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "resume",
        "id": top.id, "relevance": still_relevant})
    continue_step(top)

# Why this works: resumption isn't automatic -- the agent re-checks
# whether the frame still matters before continuing, and the depth
# cap plus TTL ensure suspensions can't accumulate indefinitely.
# Related: see `agent-resumption`, `decision-log`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: when a frame is too stale or its relevance confidence is too low, hand off to `conversation-handoff` so the next agent (or the user) sees a structured snapshot rather than picking up cold.
examples.code:
RELEVANCE_HANDOFF_FLOOR = 0.25

function resume_with_handoff():
    if not stack: return
    top = stack[-1]
    age = now() - top.opened_at
    relevance = model.estimate_certainty(
        f"is goal '{top.goal}' still worth resuming given current context?")

    if age > FRAME_TTL and relevance < RELEVANCE_HANDOFF_FLOOR:
        # introspection: 'this thread is too cold for me to safely
        # continue alone' -> structured handoff instead of guessing
        snapshot = {
            "frame_id":  top.id,
            "goal":      top.goal,
            "steps_done": top.done,
            "steps_total": top.steps,
            "age_at_handoff": age,
            "relevance_estimate": relevance,
            "opened_at": top.opened_at,
            "last_known_context": recent_context_window(),
        }
        handoff.emit(
            target="successor_or_user",
            reason="frame_too_cold_for_self_resume",
            snapshot=snapshot)
        stack.pop(); persist(stack); return

    if relevance < 0.5:
        say(f"'{top.goal}' may be stale - resuming with reduced confidence")
    else:
        say(f"back to '{top.goal}' - step {top.done+1}/{top.steps}")
    continue_step(top)

# Why this works: the introspective question 'do I actually still know
# what I was doing here?' becomes the trigger for a structured handoff,
# so a cold frame leaves a typed snapshot for whoever (human or agent)
# picks it up instead of being silently dropped or badly resumed.
# Related: see `conversation-handoff`, `agent-resumption`.

patterns.intent: Let the agent drop a note for its own future self at a specified time so context hands off across runs.

examples.intent: A tool appends to a persistent queue; each tick drains due entries into the next prompt as a SYSTEM line; fired entries are marked once.
examples.code:
queue_file = "scheduled-thoughts.jsonl"

tool schedule_future_thought(when, content, intent):
    fires_at = parse_when(when)             # ISO or +1h / +2d
    append_jsonl(queue_file, {
        "id": uuid(), "set_at": now(), "fires_at": fires_at,
        "content": content, "intent": intent, "fired": False,
    })
    return "scheduled"

function drain_due_into_prompt(now):
    due = []
    for entry in read_jsonl(queue_file):
        if entry.fired or entry.fires_at > now: continue
        due.append(entry)
        entry.fired = True
    rewrite_jsonl(queue_file)
    return [
        f"[SYSTEM: scheduled note from past-self "
        f"(set {e.set_at}, fires {e.fires_at}): {e.content}]"
        for e in due
    ]

# each tick:
prompt = [system_main] + drain_due_into_prompt(now()) + [user_msg]

examples.intent: Production variant: each scheduled memo records the past-self's confidence at scheduling time; when fired, the future-self checks whether the original rationale still holds, with a depth cap preventing memos from recursively scheduling more memos.
examples.code:
queue_file        = "scheduled-thoughts.jsonl"
MEMO_LOG          = "memo_calibration.jsonl"
MAX_CHAIN_DEPTH   = 3                  # bound recursive scheduling
_schedule_depth   = 0

tool schedule_future_thought(when, content, intent):
    global _schedule_depth
    if _schedule_depth >= MAX_CHAIN_DEPTH:
        return "refused: chain_depth_exceeded"
    fires_at = parse_when(when)
    confidence_now = model.estimate_certainty(content)
    entry = {
        "id": uuid(), "set_at": now(), "fires_at": fires_at,
        "content": content, "intent": intent,
        "past_self_confidence": confidence_now,
        "chain_depth": _schedule_depth + 1,
        "fired": False,
    }
    append_jsonl(queue_file, entry)
    append_jsonl(MEMO_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "scheduled",
        "id": entry.id, "confidence": confidence_now})
    return "scheduled"

function drain_due_into_prompt(now):
    out = []
    for e in read_jsonl(queue_file):
        if e.fired or e.fires_at > now: continue
        e.fired = True
        # introspect on firing: 'is past-self's note still relevant?'
        future_conf = model.estimate_certainty(
            f"is this still worth attending to NOW? {e.content}")
        append_jsonl(MEMO_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "fired",
            "id": e.id,
            "past_confidence": e.past_self_confidence,
            "future_confidence": future_conf})
        if future_conf < 0.25:
            # 'past-self was sure but I'm not' -> note but don't act
            out.append(f"[SYSTEM: stale memo from past-self skipped: {e.intent}]")
        else:
            out.append(f"[SYSTEM: scheduled note from past-self "
                       f"(set {e.set_at}, fires {e.fires_at}, "
                       f"past_conf={e.past_self_confidence:.2f}): {e.content}]")
    rewrite_jsonl(queue_file)
    return out

function tick_with_memos():
    global _schedule_depth
    _schedule_depth = max((e.chain_depth for e in due_memos()), default=0)
    prompt = [system_main] + drain_due_into_prompt(now()) + [user_msg]
    return model.complete(prompt)

# Why this works: future-self isn't bound by past-self's certainty --
# every fired memo gets a fresh confidence check, and chained memos
# can't snowball because depth is propagated and capped.
# Related: see `decision-log`, `provenance-ledger`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: memos that fire compose with `salience-triggered-output` so the agent only surfaces them to the user when the firing context is salient, otherwise stores them quietly in the thought stream.
examples.code:
function drain_with_salience_routing(now, ctx):
    for e in due_memos(now):
        e.fired = True
        # introspection: 'should the USER see this fire right now?'
        relevance_now = model.estimate_certainty(
            f"is this memo content relevant to user's current focus? {e.content}")
        salience      = salience_attention.score(
            topic=e.content, current_focus=ctx.user_focus)
        impact        = relevance_now * salience

        if impact >= 0.6:
            # high impact -> user-visible salience-triggered surface
            salience_triggered_output.emit(
                kind="past_self_memo",
                text=e.content,
                rationale=f"past-intent: {e.intent}")
        elif impact >= 0.25:
            # mid impact -> agent's working thought stream only
            append_thought(
                f"[memo from past-self ({e.intent})]: {e.content}")
        else:
            # low impact -> log and let it pass; user not interrupted
            append_jsonl("memo_quietly_passed.jsonl",
                {"id": e.id, "impact": impact, "content": e.content})

    rewrite_jsonl(queue_file)

# Why this works: the past-self's commitment doesn't unconditionally
# interrupt the user -- the firing agent introspects on current relevance
# and salience together, and routes the memo to the right surface
# (user, thought stream, or quiet log) accordingly.
# Related: see `salience-triggered-output`, `decision-log`.

patterns.intent: A bounded runtime mode where external I/O pauses but internal inference accelerates with a collapsed tool surface.

examples.intent: A mode-state file swaps the tool palette to inner-only, speeds up cadence, redirects output to a private journal, and auto-exits on a wall-clock budget.

Production variant: entering meditation records the agent's stated reason and confidence in needing the mode; every public-write attempt during meditation is logged as a typed refusal; auto-exit is bounded and a separate kill-file gives the operator a no-recursion exit path.
examples.code:
INNER_ALLOWLIST = {"inner_dialogue", "recall",
                   "register_affect", "exit_meditation"}
MODE_FILE       = "state/meditation.json"
KILL_FILE       = "state/meditation.kill"
DEFAULT_BUDGET  = 15 * MIN
INNER_TICK      = 10 * SEC
MED_LOG         = "meditation_audit.jsonl"

function enter_meditation(reason, budget=DEFAULT_BUDGET):
    confidence = model.estimate_certainty(
        f"do I need to drop external surface now? reason: {reason}")
    if confidence < 0.5:
        # 'I'm not sure I need this' -> don't enter, log the attempt
        append_jsonl(MED_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "refused_self_entry",
            "reason": reason, "confidence": confidence})
        return
    write_json(MODE_FILE, {"on": True, "started_at": now(),
        "budget": budget, "reason": reason, "confidence": confidence})
    append_jsonl(MED_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "enter",
        "reason": reason, "confidence": confidence, "budget": budget})

function in_meditation():
    if file_exists(KILL_FILE):                # operator kill path
        clear(MODE_FILE); remove(KILL_FILE); return False
    s = read_json(MODE_FILE, default={"on": False})
    if not s.on: return False
    if now() - s.started_at > s.budget:
        append_jsonl(MED_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "auto_exit"})
        clear(MODE_FILE); return False
    return True

function dispatch(call):
    if in_meditation():
        if call.tool not in INNER_ALLOWLIST:
            append_jsonl(MED_LOG, {"ts": now(), "event": "public_blocked",
                "tool": call.tool})
            return {"error": "public_write_blocked_in_meditation"}
        out = run_inner(call)
        append("journal/inner-dialogue/" + today(), out)
        return out
    return run_normal(call)

# Why this works: entering, exiting, and every blocked tool call leave
# typed audit records, the agent's self-check gates entry against a
# spurious meditation request, and the operator always has a kill
# path that doesn't depend on the agent itself acting.
# Related: see `circuit-breaker`, `provenance-ledger`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: meditation composes with `mode-adaptive-cadence` so the inner-tick rhythm is delegated to the cadence module, and exiting meditation restores the previously-active cadence mode rather than defaulting to idle.
examples.code:
function enter_meditation_with_cadence(reason, budget=DEFAULT_BUDGET):
    # introspective gate: do I really need to drop external surface?
    if model.estimate_certainty(
           f"meditation justified by: {reason}") < 0.5:
        return

    prior_cadence = cadence.current_mode_snapshot()
    write_json(MODE_FILE, {
        "on": True, "started_at": now(), "budget": budget,
        "reason": reason, "prior_cadence": prior_cadence})

    # hand cadence control to a meditation-only fast rhythm
    cadence.push_mode(
        name="meditation_inner",
        interval=INNER_TICK,
        lock_until=now() + budget)

function exit_meditation():
    s = read_json(MODE_FILE, default={"on": False})
    if not s.on: return
    clear(MODE_FILE)
    # restore cadence to whatever was running before
    cadence.pop_mode(restore_to=s.prior_cadence)
    ledger.append({"ts": now(), "event": "meditation_exit",
        "restored_mode": s.prior_cadence.name})

function tick_interval():
    return cadence.next_interval()           # cadence owns timing

# Why this works: meditation no longer has its own cadence logic; it
# pushes a typed cadence mode while it owns the loop and pops it on
# exit, so the agent comes back to the rhythm it was actually in
# instead of falling into idle by default.
# Related: see `mode-adaptive-cadence`, `subject-first-agent-architecture`.

patterns.intent: Vary the agent's loop interval based on salience so the agent thinks faster when something is happening.

examples.intent: Two modes with bounded intervals; a salience score promotes to intense for N ticks; lock-in cannot be self-extended without an external trigger.

Production variant: every mode transition records the agent's confidence in the salience score that triggered it; low-confidence promotions are downgraded to a 'tentative' state that auto-reverts faster than full intense lock-in.
examples.code:
MODES = {"idle": 60, "intense": 15, "tentative_intense": 30}
FLOOR, CEIL              = 10, 600
INTENSE_LOCK_TICKS       = 12
TENTATIVE_LOCK_TICKS     = 3
SALIENCE_THRESHOLD       = 0.6
SALIENCE_CONF_FOR_FULL   = 0.7
CAD_LOG                  = "cadence_audit.jsonl"

state = load_json("cadence.json",
    default={"mode": "idle", "lock_ticks_left": 0})

function next_interval(tick_result, external_impulse):
    s          = max(tick_result.novelty, external_impulse)
    conf_in_s  = model.estimate_certainty(f"is salience really {s:.2f}?")

    if external_impulse > 0.0:
        target, lock = "intense", INTENSE_LOCK_TICKS
    elif s >= SALIENCE_THRESHOLD and conf_in_s >= SALIENCE_CONF_FOR_FULL:
        target, lock = "intense", INTENSE_LOCK_TICKS
    elif s >= SALIENCE_THRESHOLD:
        # 'I'm not sure this is really salient' -> tentative only
        target, lock = "tentative_intense", TENTATIVE_LOCK_TICKS
    elif state.lock_ticks_left > 0:
        target, lock = state.mode, state.lock_ticks_left - 1
    else:
        target, lock = "idle", 0

    if target != state.mode:
        append_jsonl(CAD_LOG, {"ts": now(),
            "from": state.mode, "to": target,
            "salience": s, "salience_confidence": conf_in_s,
            "external": external_impulse})
    state.mode, state.lock_ticks_left = target, lock
    persist(state)
    return clamp(MODES[state.mode], FLOOR, CEIL)

# Why this works: the cadence module asks itself how trustworthy the
# salience signal is before locking into intense, and uncertain spikes
# only get the shorter tentative lock-in, so weak signals can't drag
# the loop into expensive fast-ticking.
# Related: see `salience-triggered-output`, `step-budget`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: cadence subscribes to `salience-triggered-output` events so the same salience the agent uses to decide whether to speak also adjusts how fast it thinks; both surfaces read the same signal once.
examples.code:
function on_salience_event(event):
    # event already carries salience score AND the agent's confidence
    # in it from the salience-triggered-output reducer
    if event.confidence < 0.3:
        # 'not sure this matters' -> neither speak nor accelerate
        log_event("salience_low_confidence", score=event.score)
        return

    if event.score >= SPEAK_THRESHOLD and event.confidence >= 0.6:
        salience_output.emit_to_user(event)

    # cadence reaction is independent of whether we spoke
    if event.score >= SALIENCE_THRESHOLD:
        cadence.promote(
            target="intense",
            lock_ticks=INTENSE_LOCK_TICKS,
            reason=f"salience event {event.id} score={event.score:.2f}")

function cadence_promote(target, lock_ticks, reason):
    if target == "intense" and state.mode != "intense":
        append_jsonl("cadence_audit.jsonl", {"ts": now(),
            "event": "promote", "reason": reason})
    state.mode = target
    state.lock_ticks_left = lock_ticks
    persist(state)

# Why this works: speaking-decisions and tempo-decisions share a single
# introspective signal (score plus confidence in score), so the agent
# can't end up speaking quickly while ticking slowly, or vice versa,
# and the audit trail correlates the two cleanly.
# Related: see `salience-triggered-output`, `salience-attention-mechanism`.

patterns.intent: Gate which short-term thoughts qualify for promotion to long-term insights by a weighted multi-axis score.

examples.intent: Six axes, each in 0..1 via a saturating curve; weights sum to one and live in config; every score event is appended to a JSONL log; deep consolidation makes the final call.
examples.code:
WEIGHTS = {                       # sum to 1.0, config-revisable
    "frequency":     0.10,
    "relevance":     0.20,
    "diversity":     0.10,
    "recency":       0.15,
    "consolidation": 0.30,        # consolidation events count more
    "conceptual":    0.15,
}
PROMOTION_THRESHOLD = 0.55

function saturate(x, k=5):        # bounded 0..1 curve
    return 1 - exp(-k * x)

function score(thought, events):
    axes = {
        "frequency":     saturate(count(events, "recall")     / 10),
        "relevance":     saturate(events.relevance_signal),
        "diversity":     saturate(events.distinct_contexts   / 5),
        "recency":       half_life_recency(thought.last_seen),
        "consolidation": saturate(count(events, "dream-survival") / 3),
        "conceptual":    saturate(events.conceptual_depth),
    }
    total = sum(WEIGHTS[a] * v for a, v in axes.items())
    append_jsonl("score_events.jsonl",
        {"thought": thought.id, "axes": axes, "total": total,
         "event_type": events.tag, "ts": now()})
    return total

function is_promotion_candidate(thought, events):
    return score(thought, events) >= PROMOTION_THRESHOLD

examples.intent: Production variant: each promotion candidate is scored with an explicit per-axis confidence; low confidence in the consolidation axis routes the thought to a 'needs-more-evidence' bucket rather than a promotion decision.
examples.code:
WEIGHTS = {"frequency":0.10, "relevance":0.20, "diversity":0.10,
           "recency":0.15, "consolidation":0.30, "conceptual":0.15}
PROMOTION_THRESHOLD       = 0.55
CONSOLIDATION_CONF_FLOOR  = 0.4
SCORE_LOG                 = "score_events.jsonl"

function saturate(x, k=5): return 1 - exp(-k * x)

function score_with_confidence(thought, events):
    axes = {
        "frequency":     (saturate(count(events, "recall")/10),
                          confidence_count(events, "recall")),
        "relevance":     (saturate(events.relevance_signal),
                          events.relevance_confidence),
        "diversity":     (saturate(events.distinct_contexts/5), 0.8),
        "recency":       (half_life_recency(thought.last_seen), 1.0),
        "consolidation": (saturate(count(events, "dream-survival")/3),
                          confidence_count(events, "dream-survival")),
        "conceptual":    (saturate(events.conceptual_depth),
                          events.conceptual_confidence),
    }
    total = sum(WEIGHTS[a] * v for a,(v,_) in axes.items())
    append_jsonl(SCORE_LOG, {"ts": now(), "thought": thought.id,
        "axes": {a: {"value": v, "confidence": c}
                  for a,(v,c) in axes.items()},
        "total": total, "event_type": events.tag})
    return total, axes

function route_candidate(thought, events):
    total, axes = score_with_confidence(thought, events)
    cons_val, cons_conf = axes["consolidation"]

    if total < PROMOTION_THRESHOLD:
        return "skip"
    if cons_conf < CONSOLIDATION_CONF_FLOOR:
        # 'I'm not sure dream-survival was real' -> hold for evidence
        return "needs_more_evidence"
    return "promote_candidate"

# Why this works: a thought above threshold but weakly supported on the
# load-bearing consolidation axis goes to a holding bucket rather than
# directly into the promotion queue, so long-term memory only grows
# from confidently-scored material.
# Related: see `dream-consolidation-cycle`, `provenance-ledger`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: scoring composes with `salience-attention-mechanism` so the relevance and recency axes are sourced from the same attention model used elsewhere; an introspective check refuses to score thoughts whose attention signal is itself stale.
examples.code:
ATTENTION_FRESHNESS_MAX = 24 * HOUR

function score_via_attention(thought, events):
    attn = salience_attention.score_for(thought.id)
    if now() - attn.last_updated > ATTENTION_FRESHNESS_MAX:
        # the agent's attention model for this thought is stale; don't
        # score it now -- ask attention to refresh first
        salience_attention.request_refresh(thought.id)
        return None

    axes = {
        "frequency":     saturate(count(events, "recall")/10),
        "relevance":     attn.relevance,         # from attention
        "diversity":     saturate(events.distinct_contexts/5),
        "recency":       attn.recency,           # from attention
        "consolidation": saturate(count(events, "dream-survival")/3),
        "conceptual":    saturate(events.conceptual_depth),
    }
    total = sum(WEIGHTS[a] * v for a,v in axes.items())
    append_jsonl("score_events.jsonl", {"ts": now(),
        "thought": thought.id, "axes": axes, "total": total,
        "attention_age": now() - attn.last_updated})
    return total

function is_promotion_candidate(thought, events):
    score = score_via_attention(thought, events)
    return score is not None and score >= PROMOTION_THRESHOLD

# Why this works: the scoring pass doesn't synthesise its own relevance
# and recency in isolation; it reads from the single attention surface
# the rest of the agent uses, and refuses to score on stale attention,
# so promotion decisions stay coherent with the agent's current focus.
# Related: see `salience-attention-mechanism`, `dream-consolidation-cycle`.

patterns.intent: Persist the agent's unresolved questions as a typed ledger so they drive the next inquiry.

examples.intent: Append-only ledger; idle tick reads top entries by curiosity*intrusiveness; intrusiveness gates ask-now versus store-quietly; closing events never edit originals.

Production variant: each ask-now decision records the agent's confidence in its own intrusiveness score, and a per-window ask-budget prevents the tension store from interrogating the user recursively.
examples.code:
ledger              = "tensions.jsonl"
CURIOSITY_FLOOR     = 0.1
DEFAULT_TTL         = 7 * DAY
ASK_NOW             = 0.7
ASK_BUDGET_PER_HOUR = 2
TENSION_LOG         = "tension_audit.jsonl"
ask_history         = bounded_deque(maxlen=32)

function can_ask_now(now):
    recent = [t for t in ask_history if t > now - HOUR]
    return len(recent) < ASK_BUDGET_PER_HOUR

function open_tension(topic, source, curiosity, intrusiveness,
                      ttl=DEFAULT_TTL):
    intr_conf = model.estimate_certainty(
        f"how disruptive really is this for the user: {topic}?")
    append_jsonl(ledger, {"kind":"open", "id": uuid(),
        "opened_at": now(), "topic": topic, "source": source,
        "curiosity": curiosity, "intrusiveness": intrusiveness,
        "intrusiveness_confidence": intr_conf,
        "expires_at": now() + ttl})

function next_move(now):
    rows = active_tensions(now)
    if not rows: return ("none", None)
    top = rows[0]
    # only consider asking if the intrusiveness estimate itself is
    # confident; otherwise store quietly
    if (top.intrusiveness >= ASK_NOW and
        top.intrusiveness_confidence >= 0.5 and
        can_ask_now(now)):
        ask_history.append(now)
        append_jsonl(TENSION_LOG, {"ts": now, "event": "ask",
            "topic": top.topic, "intr": top.intrusiveness,
            "intr_conf": top.intrusiveness_confidence})
        return ("ask_user", top)
    append_jsonl(TENSION_LOG, {"ts": now, "event": "store_quiet",
        "topic": top.topic})
    return ("store_quiet", rows[:5])

function close_tension(ref, resolution):
    append_jsonl(ledger, {"kind":"close", "ref": ref,
        "closed_at": now(), "resolution": resolution})

# Why this works: questions never get fired at the user purely because
# the agent THINKS they're intrusive -- the agent also tracks how
# confident it is in that judgment, and the rate limit prevents a
# tension storm from becoming a user-interrogation storm.
# Related: see `preoccupation-tracking`, `salience-triggered-output`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: highest-curiosity tensions feed `cognitive-move-selector` as a 'revisit-tension' candidate move, and the selector's pick decides whether the next idle tick actually pursues the tension.
examples.code:
function next_idle_move(ctx):
    tensions = active_tensions(now())[:5]
    top      = tensions[0] if tensions else None

    # offer the tension to the move selector instead of acting on it
    ctx_for_selector = {
        "recent_thoughts": ctx.recent_thoughts[-3:],
        "affect_snapshot": ctx.affect_snapshot,
        "open_tension_count": len(tensions),
        "top_tension": top.topic if top else None,
        "top_curiosity": top.curiosity if top else 0.0,
    }
    move, conf = pick_move_with_confidence(ctx_for_selector)

    if move == "revisit-tension" and top and conf > 0.4:
        # introspective handoff: the selector says revisit, and is
        # confident enough -- pursue the tension this tick
        thought = model.complete(
            f"sit with the open question: {top.topic}\n"
            f"source: {top.source}; curiosity: {top.curiosity:.2f}")
        if thought.resolves_question:
            close_tension(top.id, resolution=thought.text)
        else:
            append_jsonl("tension_audit.jsonl", {"ts": now(),
                "event": "revisit_no_resolution",
                "topic": top.topic, "thought": thought.text})
    else:
        # selector chose a different move or is uncertain -> tension
        # stays in the store for a later tick
        MENU[move](ctx)

# Why this works: the tension store doesn't itself decide when to act;
# it offers the highest-curiosity question to the move selector and
# lets the selector's introspective pick decide, so tensions wait
# politely until the agent's own move-selection says it's time.
# Related: see `cognitive-move-selector`, `socratic-questioning-agent`.

patterns.intent: Generate several candidate thoughts in parallel under named voices; the same model picks the canonical one and the losers are logged.

examples.intent: One completion produces 2-3 voice-tagged candidates and a 'selected' line; the winner enters the stream, losers go to an audit log, chronically losing voices become retirement candidates.
examples.code:
VOICES = ["world-model", "critic", "prediction"]
RETIRE_WINDOW = 50
win_history = bounded_deque(maxlen=RETIRE_WINDOW)

function propose_and_select(ctx):
    prompt = (f"Produce one candidate per voice in {VOICES}, each "
              f"prefixed '[voice: <id>]'. Then output a single line "
              f"'selected: <voice>' with a one-sentence reason.\n"
              f"context: {ctx}")
    completion = model.complete(prompt)
    candidates = parse_voice_blocks(completion)        # {voice: text}
    selected_voice, reason = parse_selected(completion)

    canonical = candidates[selected_voice]
    losers = {v: t for v, t in candidates.items() if v != selected_voice}
    append_jsonl("proposer-losers.jsonl",
        {"ts": now(), "selected": selected_voice,
         "reason": reason, "losers": losers})
    win_history.append(selected_voice)
    return canonical

function retirement_candidates():
    if len(win_history) < RETIRE_WINDOW: return []
    return [v for v in VOICES if v not in set(win_history)]
    # explicit retirement decision happens elsewhere, not silently

examples.intent: Production variant: the selector emits its confidence in the pick alongside the choice; low-confidence selections fall back to a 'no canonical' state where the losers are also visible to the model, and chronic losers are flagged for retirement review.
examples.code:
VOICES         = ["world-model", "critic", "prediction"]
RETIRE_WINDOW  = 50
SELECT_CONF_OK = 0.4
LOSERS_LOG     = "proposer-losers.jsonl"
RETIRE_LOG     = "proposer-retirement.jsonl"
win_history    = bounded_deque(maxlen=RETIRE_WINDOW)

function propose_and_select(ctx):
    prompt = (f"Produce one candidate per voice in {VOICES}, each\n"
              f"prefixed '[voice: <id>]'. Then output:\n"
              f"  selected: <voice>\n"
              f"  confidence: 0..1\n"
              f"  reason: <one sentence>\n"
              f"context: {ctx}")
    completion = model.complete(prompt)
    candidates = parse_voice_blocks(completion)
    sel_voice, sel_conf, reason = parse_selection(completion)

    append_jsonl(LOSERS_LOG, {"ts": now(), "selected": sel_voice,
        "select_confidence": sel_conf, "reason": reason,
        "losers": {v:t for v,t in candidates.items() if v != sel_voice}})

    if sel_conf < SELECT_CONF_OK:
        # 'I produced candidates but I'm not sure which is canonical'
        # -> don't pick; emit all candidates as parallel proposals
        return {"canonical": None,
                "parallel": candidates,
                "reason": "select_low_confidence"}

    win_history.append(sel_voice)
    candidates_for_retirement_check()
    return {"canonical": candidates[sel_voice],
            "loser_voices": [v for v in candidates if v != sel_voice]}

function candidates_for_retirement_check():
    if len(win_history) < RETIRE_WINDOW: return
    losing = [v for v in VOICES if v not in set(win_history)]
    if losing:
        append_jsonl(RETIRE_LOG, {"ts": now(),
            "chronic_losers": losing,
            "note": "retirement decision requires explicit ratification"})

# Why this works: the proposer can introspect on its own selection step,
# and a low-confidence pick keeps disagreement visible rather than
# collapsing to a tentative canonical; chronic losers surface as a
# typed retirement candidate, not a silent drop.
# Related: see `inner-committee`, `confidence-reporting`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: proposer-losers feed `open-question-tension-store` so dissenting voices that scored close to the winner survive as questions for later reflection rather than being archived only.
examples.code:
CLOSE_MARGIN = 0.15        # winner-vs-loser distance under which the
                           # loser is treated as 'almost-canonical'

function propose_and_select_with_tensions(ctx):
    completion = model.complete(propose_prompt(ctx))
    candidates = parse_voice_blocks(completion)
    scores     = parse_per_voice_scores(completion)
    sel_voice  = argmax(scores)

    winner_score = scores[sel_voice]
    for v, t in candidates.items():
        if v == sel_voice: continue
        margin = winner_score - scores[v]

        if margin < CLOSE_MARGIN:
            # the loser was almost canonical -> introspect: is there an
            # unresolved pull here worth keeping alive?
            curiosity = 1.0 - margin / CLOSE_MARGIN
            tension_store.open(
                topic=f"alt-take[{v}]: {t[:60]}",
                source=f"proposer-margin={margin:.2f}",
                curiosity=curiosity,
                intrusiveness=0.1)
        else:
            # clearly weaker: archive as before, no tension
            append_jsonl("proposer-losers.jsonl",
                {"ts": now(), "voice": v, "text": t,
                 "margin": margin})

    win_history.append(sel_voice)
    return candidates[sel_voice]

# Why this works: a near-tie between voices isn't an audit-log entry
# the agent will never look at -- it becomes an open tension whose
# curiosity reflects how close the loser came, so dissent has a
# concrete chance to be revisited and not just remembered.
